brand_focus: Cooking tips, recipes, and marinades with AI-styled ingredient photography. Audience is Europe-first, US a strong second. Goal: grow followers fast-but-safe by making every post highly shareable (sent to a friend) and saveable.

slots.note: Tue ~18:22 CET — w1-18 is the joint top-reach slot (avg 2.5); marinade has consistently matched that reach ceiling. Retaining and shifting minute slightly to avoid robotic pattern.
Thu ~12:41 CET — w3-12 is the single highest-reach slot recorded (avg 3.0); sauce also tied for top theme reach (2.5). Pairing best slot with best theme. Minute shifted from prior 19.
Sat ~11:07 CET — replacing the low-reach w5-10 slot (avg 1.0) with a slightly later Saturday morning window. Technique swapped in here to test whether the theme performs better outside Mon evening.
Mon ~19:34 CET — w0-19 held avg reach of 2.0, on par with most slots. Quick-dinner moved here from Saturday where it posted the weakest reach (1.5). Minute adjusted from 11.

content_plan.marinade: One complete marinade built around a memorable, visual ratio (e.g. 3-2-1 oil/acid/flavour). Slide 1: bold hero shot of the finished protein with an outcome headline. Slide 2: ratio written in large, high-contrast text — screenshot-ready. Slides 3-4: one technique note each (marinating time window, a common mistake, food-safety tip). Final slide: explicit save CTA ('Save before you shop') plus a send prompt ('Tag the person who always over-marinates'). Every slide must earn its place with one discrete piece of information worth keeping.
content_plan.sauce: A versatile sauce, glaze, or dressing built on a memorisable ratio (e.g. 2-1-1 fat/acid/flavour). Slide 1: finished sauce on a clean, beautiful surface with a one-line descriptor in the caption overlay. Slide 2: ratio in bold, readable type. Slides 3-4: show the same sauce on two very different foods (e.g. grilled fish AND roasted veg) to maximise perceived versatility and DM sends. Final slide: '3 ways to use this tonight' — the explicit numbered list is the primary send trigger.
content_plan.technique: One practical, slightly surprising kitchen technique that makes someone say 'I didn't know that.' Slide 1: striking visual of the technique in action. Slide 2: the rule or ratio in plain language, large text, built to be screenshotted. Slides 3-4: before/after or common mistake vs correct method — contrast drives sends. Final slide: one-line plain-English summary and a send prompt ('Send this to someone who does it wrong'). Keep each slide to a single idea; resist adding extra steps.
content_plan.quick-dinner: A genuinely fast (≤30 min) weeknight recipe. Slide 1: finished dish with a bold time claim visible in the image ('Done in 25 min'). Slide 2: full ingredient list with quantities large enough to read without zooming (≤7 ingredients) — this is the primary save trigger. Slides 3-4: one critical technique step each with a short explanatory line. Final slide: 'Save this for tonight' with a specific occasion anchor (e.g. 'perfect reset after a long Monday'). Quantities on slide 2 must be large and unambiguous.

caption_strategy: All captions should open with a search-friendly hook in plain language (no emojis in the first line) that names the dish, technique, or ratio explicitly — e.g. 'A 3-2-1 marinade ratio that works on any protein.' This helps keyword indexing at a stage when the account has no algorithmic history. Follow with one or two sentences of context or a problem the viewer has ('You probably over-marinate without knowing it'). Close with a direct, low-friction CTA: either a save prompt or a send prompt, not both — pick the one that fits the slide content. Keep total caption length to 80-120 words. Avoid vague lifestyle language; every sentence should deliver information or utility.
hashtag_strategy: Use exactly 4-5 hashtags per post. Avoid mega-tags (over 5 M posts) entirely — they provide zero discoverability for a brand-new account. Target two mid-size niche tags (200 K–1 M posts, e.g. #marinaderecipe, #saucetips), one smaller community tag (under 200 K, e.g. #brineandbloom or a dish-specific tag), and one broad-but-relevant food keyword tag (1–3 M, e.g. #easydinnerideas). Place hashtags at the end of the caption rather than in a comment — early caption keyword density matters more right now than hashtag hiding.
learnings: All performance metrics are effectively zero across all 8 posts and all themes — reach is 1-3 (likely followers only, not non-follower distribution), sends and saves are 0. This is a cold-start signal problem, not a content quality problem: the algorithm has no engagement history to distribute from. Priority actions: (1) The w3-12 slot produced the highest raw reach (3.0) — protect it. (2) w5-10 produced the lowest reach (1.0) — dropped. (3) Hold cadence at 4/week; do not increase until at least one post records a non-zero saves_per_reach or sends_per_reach. (4) Focus captions on keyword searchability above all else.
